text:
Today, I spent much of my time bouncing ideas of an LLM (Claude) and using the LLM as a foil. I'm interested in "language" design, hence, my line of thinking went down this path...

**The interesting corollary**

If the text-as-notation choice was driven by hardware weakness — keyboards and line printers being the available I/O — then the arrival of bitmap displays and pointing devices in the late 1970s and 1980s _should_ have triggered a paradigm shift toward diagrammatic programming. It didn't, for exactly the tooling-gravity reason discussed earlier: by then, text-based languages had accumulated compilers, libraries, communities, and curricula. Smalltalk gestured toward something different. Sketchpad had already shown in 1963 what a diagrammatic computing medium could look like. But the gravity of text was already too strong.

We may be at the first moment where that gravity is genuinely weakening — not because hardware changed again, but because LLMs make it possible to build the _tooling layer_ for new notations without the decades of infrastructure investment that previously made it prohibitive.

hi guys. on the topic of thinking and especially thinking ahead, i started with the "project" in the presentation link almost a year ago, solo, because an idea sparked an avalanche of discoveries. Sadly i a m in no position to publicly disclose anything technical for security reasons.. think in terms of CRISPR. however i have this barely finished presentation which is aimed at general public. some slides are missing but all audio is there.. would love to hear you feedback.

text:
Show, don't tell :slightly_smiling_face: If LLMs make a new tooling layer possible, let's see it. I think you have enough ideas, stop bouncing them around. Show one compiler or library or curriculum every week for this diagrammatic medium.

I think you'll find that text has a lot more than fifty years of momentum. More like three thousand years. And it consists not of solutions but of evaluation. We spent years trying out alternatives before we settled on the current state. I don't think AI speeds up evaluation, even if we accept all the current hype. So you might be able to speed up three thousand years to a thousand at best.

text:
Drawings have an even longer history than text. But drawings conveying _precise_ information are very recent. I don't know about diagrams, but the person credited for having invented infographics is William Playfair, late 18th century. At that time, semi-formal textual notations were already in general use in science, and tables well established in accounting.

<https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/William_Playfair>

text: I don't see natural resources mentioned anywhere in your slides. Just some abstract notion of abundance. Have you thought about the (free) energy consumption of your post-monetary civilization?

elements.text: I don't see natural resources mentioned anywhere in your slides. Just some abstract notion of abundance. Have you thought about the (free) energy consumption of your post-monetary civilization?

text:
Love the description of your world view! Big kudos for showing and sharing this. :heart:

I think I'm on the same page wrt seeing the crooked world we live in and abundance being real (including free energy :wink:). And I see changes are happening in that direction (difficult to see if you follow mainstream media I think).
What I don't understand is, why would we need these systems then? I’m a programmer-addict. I love it so much, but technology’s place should be limited in this new world of abundance (for the record: in the current time/situation it should be limited too imo). We humans are far greater than technology. In what way will it empower us to be more, achieve more? Technology is just a tool. But in a world of abundance, a tool for what? In my opinion it can be a tool to learn. Simulations are a good example (you mentioned in the slides). We learn best from experience, but some things are difficult to experience and simulation is a great 2nd best then. For games! And technology can help store and retrieve information. Old school technology, but useful. Not as a ledger to keep track of everything that ever happened (we have the Akashic records for that). And for interactive stories of course :slightly_smiling_face:. We definitely need more stories.
Would it be needed to decide between resource usage (as the slides seem to suggest)? Between deciding what is good for the community (again, as the slides seem to suggest)? Isn't that something the collective consciousness (which includes the consciousness of the resources themselves) helps decide on its own, without the need for technology?

Why do you think we need technology for the decision making? Why do we need to keep track of so many facts?

Again, I'm with you on the world view, but I wonder whether we need such overarching technology to make it work. I'm more in favour of small malleable tools which can help us in the everyday situation. Which can be used playfully. Which make us happy while using them :smile:.
